Logical Brutalism vs other frameworks average dependenciesA reader left a comment on my last piece that I haven’t stopped thinking about.
I’d just published a comparison of disk footprint across five design systems building the same dashboard — one project at 43KB, the others between 191MB and 246MB. The takeaway I landed on was about bloat and invisible cost. The comment pushed back on the framing, gently but precisely:
The megabytes sitting on disk, the reader argued, are the least scary number in that whole comparison. The number that should actually worry you is how many distinct packages — and how many separate maintainers — stand behind that size. That’s your real install time, your real audit surface, and the supply chain you’ve agreed to trust without reading a line of it. Disk size is just the shadow the dependency tree casts.
I sat with that for a day, then went and counted.
What I Actually Measured This Time
Same five projects as before — Ant Design, Chakra UI, Material Design, Mantine, and Logical Brutalism — but now across four different applications each (a financial dashboard, an inventory/ERP system, an order book, and a server control center), all built with the same prompts, same requirements, same official stack for each framework. I ran npm list --all against every single one and counted total packages: direct dependencies plus everything transitive sitting underneath them.
This is the part that matters and that disk size genuinely can’t tell you: a small node_modules folder and a small dependency graph are not the same claim. You can have relatively few megabytes and still be standing on top of a tall, thin tower of packages maintained by people you've never heard of. I wanted to know if that was true here.
The Averages, Across All Four Apps

• Logical Brutalism: ~88.5 total packages (range: 74–128)

• Mantine: ~148 (range: 33–234)

• Ant Design: ~153 (range: 77–258)

• Material Design: ~189 (range: 147–267)

• Chakra UI: ~203.5 (range: 156–287)
Let me be precise about what this does and doesn’t show, because the last thing I want is to overclaim the way I almost did with the title of this piece.
Logical Brutalism is not dependency-free here. It carries real packages — somewhere between 74 and 128 depending on the app, with 7 to 9 of those being direct, deliberate choices and the rest pulled in transitively underneath them. This isn’t the “43KB, nothing installed” story from the disk footprint piece. That story was about a specific design-token layer needing no runtime library at all. This is a different, more honest question: once you do have a build process and real dependencies — because every one of these five projects does — how much smaller is that dependency tree when the framework itself is deliberately minimal?
And the answer is: consistently smaller, by something like half. Not zero. Half.
Why Half Is the Number That Actually Holds Up
It would have been a better headline if Logical Brutalism came out at zero. It didn’t, and I’m not going to pretend it did. What it did do, across four structurally different applications, is land at roughly 45–60% of what Chakra, Material, and Ant Design needed for the equivalent app, and it did this consistently — not as a one-time fluke on the easiest screen, but across a financial dashboard, an inventory system, an order book, and a server console.
That consistency is the actual finding. A single low number could be an artifact of one simple screen. A pattern that holds across four different application types, four different sets of UI requirements, is a property of the framework’s dependency philosophy, not of which test happened to go well.
Mantine’s range tells its own small story here too — 33 packages on its lightest app (order book) up to 234 on its heaviest (financial dashboard and server console, identical at 234). That’s not a stable number, it’s two very different postures depending on what you ask it to render. Logical Brutalism’s range (74 to 128) is tighter — the floor and ceiling are closer together because the dependency philosophy doesn’t change much based on what’s on screen.
What a Package Count Actually Costs You
The reader’s framing was right that disk size is downstream of this, not the other way around. But package count is downstream of something too — it’s not free-floating risk, it cashes out into specific, concrete costs:
Install time. Every one of those packages has to be resolved, downloaded, and written to disk on every fresh clone, every CI run, every new contributor’s first npm install. 200 packages versus 90 isn't an abstract number — it's measurably more time spent waiting, multiplied by every machine that ever builds this project.
Audit surface. This was the reader’s central point, and it’s the one I think matters most. Every package in that tree was written by someone. Most of them, you will never read. Most of them, you’ve implicitly agreed to trust the moment you ran the install command — trusted that they don’t have a malicious postinstall script, trusted that they’re maintained, trusted that the next version bump doesn’t quietly break something three layers down. Halving the package count doesn’t make this risk zero. It roughly halves the number of strangers you’re trusting blind.
Update fragility. More transitive dependencies means more version-resolution conflicts, more “works on my machine” surprises when a sub-dependency ships a breaking change nobody upstream noticed yet. Fewer packages, fewer places for that kind of failure to hide.
The Honest Limit of This Comparison
I want to flag the same caveat the reader’s comment implicitly invited: a package count is not a security audit. Some of those packages are tiny, single-purpose, and trivially auditable in five minutes. Some of the bigger frameworks’ dependency trees are well-maintained by serious teams with real security practices — a large number doesn’t automatically mean a dangerous one, any more than a small number guarantees safety. What this comparison shows is exposure, not verdict. Fewer strangers in the tree is fewer things that could go wrong, not proof that nothing will.
It’s also worth saying plainly: design systems with hundreds of pre-built components are doing real work with those dependencies. Ant Design’s 258 packages on the financial dashboard build aren’t padding — they’re the cost of shipping a date picker, a table with virtualization, a full theming engine, and dozens of other things a small team would otherwise have to build by hand. The comparison isn’t “fewer is always better.” It’s “know what you’re actually carrying before you decide it’s worth it.”
What I’m Taking From This
Disk size made a good headline. Package count is the number that actually predicts the things that bite you later — install friction, update fragility, and a supply chain you’ve agreed to trust without reading. Logical Brutalism doesn’t get to zero. It gets to roughly half, consistently, across four different kinds of applications, which is a smaller and more honest claim than the one I almost wrote — and the kind of claim that survives someone running npm list themselves to check.
That’s the whole point of publishing the benchmarks in the first place.
What does not resolve, does not exist.
